chapters.title: Square Roots and Cube Roots
topics: Observe the following perfect square numbers
Perfect square :  A rational number that is equal to the square of another rational number.
Square number : An integer that is a square of another integer.
“The numbers that have 2, 3, 7 or 8 in the units place are not perfect squares.”

We observe that if a number has 1 or 9 in the units place, then the units digit in its square number
is only 1.
If a number has 4 or 6 in the units place, then the units digit in its square is  always 6.
From this, we can generalize that the sum of first ‘n’ odd natural numbers is equal  to ‘n2’.
These numbers are called palindromic numbers or numerical palindrome.
From the following pattern find the missing numbers
Find the missing numbers using the given pattern

With the help of the above table, try to find the number of non square numbers between n2
and                 (n + 1)2.
There are ‘2n’ non square numbers between  n2 and (n + 1)2.

Generally a, b, c are three positive integers. If a2 + b2 = c2 then (a, b, c) are said to be
pythagorean triplet.

If there are no common factors other than ‘1’ among  a,b,c then the triplet (a,b,c) is called
primitive triplet.
If the area of a square is 169 cm2. What could be the side of the square?

Therefore, if a square number is expressed, as the product of two equal factors, then one thefactors is called the square root of that square number. Thus, the square root of 169 is 13.  It can
be expressed as 169 = 13 (symbol used for square root is ). Thus it is the inverse operation
of squaring.
If y2 = x then square root of x is y  ()xy=
6.5   Finding the Square root through subtraction of successive odd numbers
Finding square root is the reverse order of this pattern.
Note: If the result of this process is not zero then the given number is not a perfect square.

There  are two methods of finding the square root of the given numbers. They are
(i) Prime factorization method(ii) Division method

6.6
Finding the Square Root Through Prime Factorisation Method
6.7  Finding square root by division method
6.8  Square root of decimal numbers using division method
6.9   Estimating square roots of non perfect square numbers
These numbers 1, 8, 27, 64 …… are called cubic numbers or perfect cubes.
Observe the following pattern and complete it

Hence we can generalize that the sum of the cubes of first ‘n’  natural numbers is
equal to the square of their sum.
i.e.  13  +  23 + 33 + ……… + n3 =  (1 + 2 + 3 +.... + n)2.

Thus, if a number can be expressed as a product of three equal factors then it is said to be a
perfect cube or cubic number.

Therefore, finding
the number whose cube is known is called finding the cube root.
It is the inverse operation of cubing.

As, 43 =  64 then 4 is called cube root of  64. We write   364 4=.  The symbol  3 denotes cube root.
Hence, a number ‘ x’ is the cube root of another number y, if y = x3 then  x = 3y.

chapters.title: Exploring Geometrical Figures
topics: (a) We know that two line segments are congruent if they have same lengths.
(b) Two angles are congruent if they have same measure.
(c) Two triangles ΔABC and ΔPQR are congruent if their corresponding sides and angles are equal.

If two polygons are congruent then their corresponding sides are equal and corresonding angles are equal. Thus the two geometrical shapes are said to be congruent if they coincide with each other exactly.

Do This: 1. Identify the out line figures which are similar to the first figure.

topics: Here all the corresponding angles are right angles and are equal.Hence we conclude that “two polygons are similar if their corresponding angles are congruent and lengths of corresponding sides are proportional”.

chapters.title: Area of Plane Figures
topics: 9 . 7 Area of a Circular Path or Area of a ring

What we have discussed: Area of a trapezium =  1/2 (Sum of the lengths of parallel sides) × (Distance between them)
Area of a quadrilateral = 1/2 × length of a diagonal × Sum of the lengths of the perpendiculars from the remaining two vertices on the diagonal)
Area of a rhombus = Half of the product of diagonals.
Area of a circle = πr²where ‘r’ is the radius of the circle.
Area of a circular path (or) Area of a Ring = π(R²- r²) or π(R + r) (R-r) where R, r are radii of outer circle and inner circle respectively.
Area of a sector = (x°/360) × πr² where x° is  the angle subtended by the arc of the sector at the center of the circle and r is radius of the circle.
A = (lr)/2. Where l - length of the arc, r - radius of the circle

chapters.title: The Elements of Geometry
topics: Postulate-1  : There is a unique line that passes through the given two distinct points.
Postulate-2 :  A line segment can be extended on either side to form a line.
Postulate-3 : We can describe a circle with any centre and radius.
Postulate-4  : All right angles are equal to one another.

Postulate-5  : That, if a straight line falling on two straight lines makes the interior angles on the same side less than two right angles, the two straight lines, if produced indefinitely, meet on that side on which are the angles less than two right angles.

chapters.title: TRIANGLES
topics: Theorem-7.2 :  Angles opposite to equal sides of an isosceles triangle are equal.
Theorem-7.3 :  The sides opposite to equal angles of a triangle are equal.
Theorem-7.6 : If two sides of a triangle are unequal, the angle opposite to the longer side is larger (or greater).
Theorem -7.7 :  In any triangle, the side opposite to the larger (greater) angle is longer.
Theorem-9.8 : The sum of any two sides of a triangle is greater than the third side.

Illustrative examples: Example-1 :   ABCD  is a parallelogram and A = 60o.  Find the remaining angles.
Example-2.  In a parallelogram ABCD,  DAB  = 40o find the remaining angles of the parallelogram.

Example-4 : In  a parallelogram ABCD,  the bisectors of the adjacent A and B intersect at P. Show that APB = 90o.

Theorem- 8.1: A  diagonal of a parallelogram divides it into two congruent triangles.

Theorem-8.2: In a parallelogram, opposite sides are equal and opposite angles are equal.

Theorem-8.3: If  each pair of opposite sides of a quadrilateral is equal, then it is a parallelogram.

Theorem-8.4: In a quadrilateral, if each pair of opposite angles are equal then it is a parallelogram.

Theorem-8.5: The  diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other.

Theorem-8.6: If the diagonals of a quadrilateral bisect each other then it is a parallelogram.

Corollary-1: Show that each angle of a rectangle is a right angle.

Corollary-2: Show that the diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular to each other.

Corollary-3: In a parallelogram ABCD, if the diagonal AC bisects the angle A, then ABCD is a rhombus.

Corollary-4: Show that the diagonals of a rectangle are of equal length.

Corollary-5: Show that the angle bisectors of a parallelogram form a rectangle.

Some Illustrative examples: Example-5. AB and DC are two parallel lines and a transversal l, intersects AB  at P and DC  at R.  Prove that the bisectors of the interior angles form a rectangle.
Example-6. In a triangle ABC, AD is the median drawn on the side BC is produced to E such that AD = ED prove that ABEC is a parallelogram.

Theorem-8.7: The line segment joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and also half of it.

Theorem-12.4 : The angle subtended by an arc at the centre of a circle is double the angle subtended by it at any point on the remaining circle.

Theorem-12.5 :  If a line segment joining two points, subtends equal angles at two other points lying on
the same side of the line then these, the four points lie on a circle ( i.e. they are concyclic)
Theorem-12.6 :  The opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary.
Theorem-12.7 :  If the sum of any pair of opposite angles in a quadrilateral is 180o, then it is cyclic.

Possible relations between two lines in: The two lines may intersect at one point.

topics: Consistent and independent pair of linear equations
Consistent and dependent pair of linear equations in two variables

Example-1. Check whether the given pair of equations represent intersecting or parallel or coincident lines. Find the solution if the lines are intersecting.
Example-2. Check whether the following pair of equations is consistent. Verify by a graphical representation.
Example-3. Check whether the system of equations 2x-3y = 5 and 4x-6y = 15 are consistent. Also verify by graphical representation.

Example-4. In a garden there are some bees and flowers. If one bee sits on each flower then one bee will be left. If two bees sit on each flower, one flower will be left. Find the number of bees and number of flowers.

Example-5. The perimeter of a rectangular plot is 32m. If the length is increased by 2m and the breadth is decreased by 1m, the area of the plot remains the same. Find the length and breadth of the plot.
4.3Algebraic Methods of Finding the Solutions for a Pair of Linear Equations
Step-1 : In one of the equations, express one variable in terms of the other variable.
Step-2 : Substitute the value of y obtained in step 1 in the second equation.
Step-3 : Simplify the equation obtained in step 2 and find the value of x.
Step-4 : Substitute the value of x obtained in step 3 in the equation of step 1 and solve for y.
Step-5 : Check the obtained solution by substituting the values of x and y in both the original equations.
Example-6. Solve the given pair of equations using substitution method.
Step-1 : Write both the equations in the form of ax + by = c.
Step-2 : Make the coefficients of one of the variables, equal by multiplying each equation by suitable real numbers.
Step-3 : If the variable to be eliminated has the same sign in both equations, subtract one equation from the other to get an equation in one variable. If they have opposite signs then add.
Step-4 : Solve the equation for the remaining variable.
Step-5 : Substitute the value of this variable in any one of the original equations and find the value of the eliminated variable.
Example-7. Solve the following pair of linear equations using elimination method.

Example-8. Rubina went to the bank to withdraw `2000. She asked the cashier to give the cash in `50 and `100 notes only. She got 25 notes in all. Can you tell how many notes each of `50 and `100 she received?

Example-9. In a competitive exam, 3 marks were awarded for every correct answer and 1 mark was deducted for every wrong answer. Madhu scored 40 marks in this exam. Had 4 marks been awarded for each correct answer and 2 marks deducted for each incorrect answer, Madhu would have scored 50 marks. How many questions were there in the test? (Madhu had attempted all questions.)

Example-10. Mary told her daughter, "Seven years ago, I was seven times as old as you were then. Also, three years from now, I shall be three times as old as you will be." Find the present age of Mary and her daughter.

Example-11. A publisher is planned to produce a new textbook. The fixed costs (reviewing, editing, typesetting and so on) are `320000. Besides that, he also spends another `31.25 in producing each book. The wholesale price (the amount received by the publisher) is `43.75 per book. How many books must the publisher sell to break even, i.e., so that the cost of production will equal revenues?
4.4 Equations Reducible to a Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables
Example-12. Solve the following pair of equations. 2/x + 3/y = 13 and 5/x - 4/y = -2

Example-13. Kavitha thought of constructing 2 more rooms in her house. She enquired about the labour and time estimates. She came to know that 6 men and 8 women could finish this work in 14 days. But she wished to complete that work in only 10 days. When she enquired elsewhere, she was told that 8 men and 12 women could finish the work in 10 days. Find out how much time would be taken to finish the work if one man or one woman worked alone.

Example-14. A man travels 370 km partly by train and partly by car. If he covers 250 km by train and the rest by car, it takes him 4 hours. But if he travels 130 km by train and the rest by car, it takes 18 minutes more. Find the speed of the train and that of the car.

chapters.title: Similar Triangles
topics: Scale or scale factor (or) representative factor

8.3.1 Basic Proportionality Theorem (Tha: Theorem-8.1  : If a line is drawn parallel to one side of a triangle to intersect the other two sides in distinct points, then the other two sides are divided in the same ratio.
Theorem-8.2 :  If a line divides two sides of a triangle in the same ratio, then the line is parallel to the third side.

topics: Construction : Division of a line segment (using Thales theorem)

8.4.1 AAA Criterion for Similarity of T: Theorem-8.3 :  In two triangles, if corresponding angles are equal, then their corresponding sides are in proportion and hence the triangles are similar.

8.4.2. SSS Criterion for Similarity of: Theorem-8.4 :  In two triangles, if corresponding sides are proportional, then their corresponding angles are equal and hence the triangles are similar.

8.4.3 SAS Criterion for Similarity of T: Theorem-8.5 :  If one angle of a triangle is equal to one angle of the other triangle and the including sides of these angles are proportional, then the two triangles are similar.

topics: Construction : To construct a triangle similar to a given triangle as per given scale factor.
Theorem-8.6 :  The ratio of the areas of two similar triangles is equal to the ratio of the squares of their corresponding sides.

Theorem-8.7 :  If a perpendicular is drawn from the vertex of the right angle of a right triangle to the hypotenuse, then the triangles on both sides of the perpendicular are similar to the whole triangle and to each other.

8.6.1 Baudhayana Theorem (Pythagoras Th: Theorem-8.8 :  In a right triangle, the square of  length of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of lengths of the other two sides.

Theorem-8.9 :  In a triangle, if square of the length of one side is equal to the sum of squares of the lengths of the other two sides, then the angle opposite to the first side is a right angle and the triangle is a right angled triangle.

chapters.title: Applications of Trigonometry
topics: Example-1.   The top of a clock tower is observed at angle of elevation of  and the foot of the tower is at the distance of d meters from the observer.  Draw the diagram for this data.

Example-2.   Rinky observes a flower on the ground from the balcony of the first floor of a building at an angle of depression . The height of the first floor of the building is x meters. Draw the diagram for this data.

Example-3.   A large balloon has been tied with a rope and it is floating in the air.  A person has observed the balloon from the top of a building at angle of elevation of 1 and foot of the rope at an angle of depression of  2.  The height of the building is h feet.  Draw the diagram for this data.
Example-4.    A boy observed the top of an electric pole at an angle of elevation of  60º when the observation point is 8 meters away from the foot of the pole. Find the height of the pole.

Example-5.   From a helicopter, Rajender observes a person standing on the ground at an angle of depression 45º.  If the helicopter is flying at a height of 500 m from the ground, what is the distance of the person from Rajender?
Example-6.   Two men on either side of a temple of 30 meter height observe its top at the angles of elevation 30º and 60º respectively. Find the distance between the two men.

Example-7.   A straight highway leads to the foot of a tower.  Ramaiah standing at the top of the tower observes a car at an angle of depression of 30º. The car is approaching the foot of the tower with a uniform speed.  Six seconds later, the angle of depression of the car is found to be 60º.  Find the time taken by the car to reach the foot of the tower from this point.
